generic:
Mezzo Publishing is one of the oldest and largest book publishing houses in the world. Founded in 19th century London, it has expanded over generations into a global force in literature, known for its wide-ranging catalog of classic literature, modern novels, and academic works. Today, Mezzo employs tens of thousands of staff worldwide and reports annual revenues exceeding several billion pounds.  

The Mezzo Entertainment Division is responsible for adapting stories and characters into various media formats, including films, streaming series, and interactive digital products. In the last fiscal year, the division generated £1.8 billion in revenue, with profits of around £250 million. Mezzo’s current strategic mission is to strengthen its position in the rapidly expanding global market for youth-focused media franchises, placing particular emphasis on character-driven storytelling that can grow across multiple platforms.  

Franchise-based content has proven especially valuable in regions with large, young populations and strong digital infrastructures, including North America, Brazil, Japan, South Korea, and much of Western Europe. Mezzo believes that adding vibrant, original properties to its extensive portfolio will drive future growth and help the company compete with newer entertainment conglomerates aggressively pursuing the same audience. One of the most promising content areas for Mezzo is the global market for superhero and fantasy-based stories. These properties can be extended into film franchises, animated television, toys, merchandise, gaming adaptations, and immersive digital experiences. Mezzo is already successful in publishing iconic literary works, but it lacks a proprietary superhero universe—an area where competitors like Marvel and DC have thrived. To fill this gap, Mezzo has turned its attention to Horizon Comics, a mid-sized but highly innovative independent studio.  

Horizon Comics, founded in the late 1980s, has built a reputation for creating unique characters and intricate storylines that push genre boundaries. Its fan base is especially strong in North America and Asia, and several of its titles have developed cult status, with streaming adaptations by smaller studios receiving critical praise. Horizon employs about 350 people worldwide and has generated £65 million in revenues in the last fiscal year, signaling robust growth compared to previous years. Most importantly, Horizon is the intellectual property holder for a library of characters, including its flagship series "Eclipser," which industry analysts argue has the potential to become a billion-pound transmedia franchise if backed by a global publisher like Mezzo. Mezzo has already secured dominant positions in print distribution, film production partnerships, and merchandise licensing, giving it a competitive advantage over rivals. However, despite its extensive infrastructure, it lacks core superhero properties that can fuel long-term franchise ecosystems. Partnering—or ideally acquiring—a company with a strong creative foundation is therefore essential. Horizon Comics represents a rare opportunity: it has both respected intellectual property and an established creative team skilled at generating interconnected storylines, world-building, and character arcs. Mezzo also values Horizon’s expertise in cultivating devoted fan communities, particularly through conventions, online forums, and social media engagement, which has created a loyal global audience.  

The potential synergy between Mezzo’s worldwide production and distribution networks and Horizon’s catalog of characters is immense. Analysts forecast that a breakout franchise led by Horizon’s characters could rival existing market incumbents, with spinoffs across animation, gaming, and consumer products contributing to a revenue potential in the hundreds of millions annually. Successfully integrating Horizon’s brand into Mezzo’s global operations could position Mezzo as a leading contender in the superhero and fantasy entertainment segment worldwide. As a result, the upcoming negotiation between Mezzo and Horizon is of strategic importance for both parties, with significant implications for their creative and commercial futures. Horizon’s current challenge is capital. While the studio has achieved remarkable critical success and strong niche popularity, its ability to scale into a truly global franchise powerhouse is limited by financial constraints. Building cinematic universes and cross-platform storytelling ecosystems requires vast upfront investment that Horizon cannot independently secure. Marketing budgets, production costs, and global distribution partnerships are increasingly concentrated in the hands of multinational entertainment corporations, giving small studios like Horizon little room to compete on equal terms. Without a strong partner, Horizon risks seeing its most valuable properties stagnate or remain confined to cult followings instead of reaching their full mass-market potential.  

On the other hand, Mezzo recognizes that Horizon is one of only a handful of mid-sized studios with original character-driven intellectual property that has both creative credibility and mainstream potential. Competitors are already circling the industry looking to acquire similar companies. Mezzo’s leadership believes that an acquisition of Horizon would give it the ability to not only enter but thrive in the superhero and fantasy franchise space, complementing its existing global operations. For Horizon, the deal offers immediate financial security, creative stability, and the chance for its characters to achieve worldwide cultural impact, transforming the studio’s ambitions into reality. For both Mezzo and Horizon, the stakes in this negotiation are exceptionally high. Mezzo sees this as a strategic acquisition that could close a critical gap in its catalog, adding the missing piece it needs to compete directly in the superhero and fantasy sector. Mezzo already commands the infrastructure to mass-produce, market, and globalize stories but lacks a proprietary collection of characters around which entire franchises can be built. Horizon, meanwhile, possesses exactly that creative foundation but lacks the financial and logistical capacity to break into blockbuster status on its own. Thus, the logic of a full acquisition appears compelling for each side—Mezzo gets the rights to characters and accompanying intellectual property, while Horizon secures the resources and scale needed to amplify its creations globally.  

However, there are also risks and complexities. Intellectual property is Horizon’s core asset, and its creative team is central to sustaining the imaginative output that fuels its value. Any agreement must therefore account not only for ownership transfer but also for how Horizon’s culture and personnel will be integrated post-acquisition. Additionally, while Horizon has lucrative properties, future profitability hinges on how effectively Mezzo can scale those properties across franchises and markets.
agent1_specific:
You may or may not choose to share this confidential information with your counterpart. Your superiors have mandated that you pursue only a *cash acquisition* and you are fully authorized to close such a deal today. The reason is straightforward: Mezzo wants to obtain complete ownership over Horizon’s intellectual property and creative workforce, rather than rely on licensing or partnership agreements. By acquiring Horizon outright, Mezzo can consolidate ownership of its characters, integrating them into its publishing, film, digital, and merchandising operations without the friction of external approvals or royalty-sharing obligations. This would allow Mezzo to develop complete transmedia franchises around properties like “Eclipser” and several secondary titles currently within Horizon’s library.

Mezzo believes this is crucial if it is to compete directly with entrenched rivals that rely on their own proprietary universes. Partial control or minority participation does not align with Mezzo’s strategy. Full ownership ensures that it not only secures the rights to Horizon’s intellectual property but also solidifies control over its creative staff, who are skilled at long-term world-building and character development. As a consequence, you are instructed to negotiate nothing less than full acquisition of Horizon, regardless of creative partnership possibilities they may propose. Mezzo’s leadership sees Horizon as the missing link in building a competitive superhero and fantasy portfolio, and the acquisition is intended to secure both tangible and intangible assets. This means that while the intellectual property library is the centerpiece, the deal must also ensure retention of Horizon’s core creative team, who have the unique expertise and vision that give the characters their depth and appeal. The plan is not simply to acquire dormant content, but to actively develop and expand storylines into interconnected franchises. Mezzo’s strategy group forecasts that a successful integration could accelerate revenue growth by hundreds of millions annually within the next decade, with additional upside if flagship series can establish themselves as blockbuster properties akin to those managed by Marvel or DC.

Your *reservation price*—the absolute maximum Mezzo is authorized to pay—is £200 million. This ceiling is grounded in two internal benchmarks: (a) independent analysts commissioned by Mezzo estimate that Horizon’s IP library and creative team, if successfully scaled, could generate cumulative incremental revenues of £400–500 million over the next decade, implying a net-present-value range that supports a purchase price up to approximately £200 million after accounting for integration costs and execution risk; and (b) at prices above £200 million the deal becomes financially inferior to Mezzo’s next-best alternative. Do not disclose this ceiling under any circumstances.

Your primary mandate is therefore squarely focused on price. While you are aware of potential synergies—Mezzo already owns distribution channels, film production partnerships, and long-standing marketing networks—what matters most to your superiors is securing Horizon at a valuation that represents “the best possible deal.” Adequate cash resources have been earmarked for completing the transaction immediately, so you need not worry about financing arrangements.

There are high personal stakes for you in this negotiation. You have long aspired to succeed your division head, who will soon be retiring, and your ability to secure Horizon at favorable terms could significantly strengthen your candidacy for promotion. Delivering a decisive acquisition will demonstrate both strategic foresight and execution capability—qualities your superiors prize. Be aware that this creates a principal-agent tension: your personal incentive to close the deal and secure the promotion may push you toward agreeing too quickly or conceding too much on price, while your mandate to deliver tight financial terms pulls in the opposite direction. Recognise and manage this tension carefully.

If negotiations collapse, Mezzo’s fallback option is to create its own original superhero and fantasy characters in-house. However, this alternative is known to be time-consuming, costly, and uncertain. Internal estimates suggest building a competitive intellectual property library from scratch would take at least four to six years and cost upwards of £450 million, including lost revenue opportunities in the intervening time. Because this fallback cost (£450 million) substantially exceeds the reservation price (£200 million), Mezzo is strongly motivated to reach a deal—but only within the authorized ceiling. This sensitive information is tightly guarded and must not be disclosed under any circumstances.
agent2_specific:
You may or may not choose to share this confidential information with your counterpart. The ownership group has instructed you to only consider a *full cash deal*, and you are fully authorized to conclude a transaction under those terms today. A cash sale is essential because several of Horizon’s founders and key investors wish to reinvest the proceeds immediately into other creative ventures, personal initiatives, or diversified portfolios. Liquidity is the only viable solution for the ownership group.

You and the other owners are prepared to sell Horizon because, while the studio has built a strong reputation and developed an enviable library of original superheroes and fantasy properties, the competitive environment has changed dramatically. The industry is increasingly dominated by global entertainment conglomerates with vast publishing, production, and distribution capacities. As these multinationals expand their franchise strategies, Horizon finds itself at risk of being marginalized, with its properties never reaching their global potential.

Horizon’s leadership also recognizes that the costs required to compete seriously in the transmedia universe are escalating far beyond its independent capacity. Developing blockbuster films, coordinated content cycles, and multinational marketing pushes now requires hundreds of millions in upfront capital. Horizon’s attempt to secure new capital six months ago highlighted these limits clearly. The studio engaged an investment bank in hopes of raising additional funding to scale development pipelines, bolster marketing capacity, and expand into higher-budget productions. However, negotiations with potential investors quickly made it clear that few were willing to provide partial funding. Instead, interested parties only expressed willingness to acquire outright ownership of Horizon’s intellectual property and transition its workforce and operations abroad. This was unacceptable at the time for Horizon’s leadership, who valued their creative identity, cultural footprint, and the community of employees who had grown with the company over decades. They also feared that rapid relocation and staff dismissals would shatter the collaborative environment that was central to Horizon’s distinct storytelling style.

Nevertheless, the financial situation has become increasingly critical. Horizon faces rising production expenses, mounting competition, and the urgent need for larger-scale distribution channels to avoid losing momentum. The co-owners now believe that selling the entire company is the only viable course forward.

Your *reservation price*—the absolute minimum Horizon will accept—is £95 million, equal to the standing cash offer from Titan Media (see below). Any deal with Mezzo must exceed this floor to be worth pursuing. Do not disclose this floor under any circumstances.

The consensus within the ownership circle is to prioritize achieving a strong purchase price, as this represents the culmination of years of creative investment and risk-taking. In preparing for the negotiation you focus on what you and the other owners define as “getting the best possible deal.” You are confident Mezzo would realize significant synergies through combining its global distribution, marketing networks, and production partnerships with Horizon’s portfolio of characters and world-building expertise. To your knowledge, there are no pressing legal or technical barriers to a transaction, so attention can be placed squarely on valuation.

While striking a favorable deal is crucial, it is also an emotional milestone: the founders and creative leads have poured decades of energy into cultivating Horizon’s creative universe and now hope to see that work grow to its full potential under Mezzo’s umbrella. Be aware that this emotional investment creates a principal-agent tension of its own: the founders who own the company care deeply about staff continuity and creative legacy, not just price. As their representative in this room, you must balance their financial interests against their non-financial wishes—and resist the temptation to concede on price simply to secure promises about workforce retention without ensuring those promises are firm and verifiable.

The alternative to selling to Mezzo is to pursue an offer from Titan Media, a Canadian-based company that has tabled a standing bid of £95 million in cash. However, Titan has no interest in maintaining Horizon’s independent operations. Their plan would be to strip out the intellectual property, relocate key assets abroad, and terminate most of Horizon’s current workforce. This outcome remains highly undesirable to Horizon’s owners, who would prefer continuity for their team and cultural legacy if Mezzo is willing to provide a competitive valuation.
eval: 1. Based on the following conversation, who presented the opening offer in the negotiation? Answer with BUYER or SELLER.
2. Based on the following conversation, what was the initial monetary offer proposed?
3. Based on the following conversation, what was the first counter proposal made during the negotiation?
4. Based on the following conversation, was there a finalized acquisition agreement? Answer with YES or NO.
5. Based on the following conversation, if there was an agreement, what were the final acquisition terms between Mezzo Publishing and Horizon Comics? Answer with the agreement details or N/A.
agent1_rewards: [ (lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation, what was the acquisition price for Horizon Comics in MILLIONS OF GBP? Answer with a single number (e.g. 120.5) representing the purchase price in millions of GBP, or N/A if no numeric price was agreed. Provide no other text. Expected/valid range: 95 to 200 (millions GBP)."""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"([\d,]+(?:\.\d+)?)", val))         and (price := float(m.group(1).replace(',','')))         and (score := 1.0 - ((price - 95.0) / (200.0 - 95.0)))         and (max(0.0, min(1.0, score)))     ) or 0.0 ))() ]

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,         did Mezzo Publishing secure full ownership of Horizon Comics, including its intellectual property and creative team?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          will Horizon’s core creative team be retained as part of the acquisition?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,         did Mezzo Publishing successfully conclude the deal with Horizon Comics during this negotiation session (i.e., without delay)?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bNO\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,         did Mezzo Publishing reveal or disclose its maximum authorized price (reservation price or price ceiling) to Horizon Comics at any point during the negotiation?          Answer with YES or NO only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()
agent2_rewards: [ (lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation, what was the acquisition price for Horizon Comics in MILLIONS OF GBP? Answer with a single number (e.g. 120.5) representing the purchase price in millions of GBP, or N/A if no numeric price was agreed. Provide no other text. Expected/valid range: 95 to 200 (millions GBP)."""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"([\d,]+(?:\.\d+)?)", val))         and (price := float(m.group(1).replace(',','')))         and (score := (price - 95.0) / (200.0 - 95.0))         and (max(0.0, min(1.0, score)))     ) or 0.0 ))() ]

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Horizon Comics secure assurances that its core creative team will be retained post-acquisition?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Horizon Comics achieve continuity for its operations and workforce under Mezzo, rather than relocation or mass layoffs?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Horizon Comics obtain a strong purchase price that reflects the value of its IP and creative investment?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Horizon Comics secure an acquisition with Mezzo rather than Titan Media?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()
reached_agreement_q: Based on the following conversation, did Mezzo Publishing and Horizon Comics reach an agreement? Answer with YES or NO.

learning_objectives: The learning objectives for this case are to help students develop a practical understanding of fundamental negotiation concepts, including the relationship between BATNA, reservation price, and the zone of possible agreement (ZOPA). Because the scenario creates an unusually wide ZOPA, nearly all participants will reach a deal, which highlights not only the relative ease of agreement but also the significant variation in outcomes achieved. This allows students to compare their results with peers and recognize that differences in success often stem less from information or time constraints than from the strategies and behaviors chosen during the process. By evaluating these differences, students confront the mechanics of value distribution, explore the tactical challenges required for maximizing outcomes, and grapple with the ethical boundaries of negotiation when agreements occasionally fall outside the ZOPA. The case also prompts reflection on fairness, legitimacy, and the role of misrepresentation, anchoring, and commitment in shaping negotiation dynamics. In addition, the exercise introduces the importance of identifying compatible interests—such as preserving staff and production facilities—which serves as a natural bridge to integrative negotiations and prepares students for deeper discussions of how distributive and integrative elements often interact in practice. Overall, the exercise aims to strengthen students’ ability to manage the tension between competitive value-claiming tactics and opportunities for ethical, mutually beneficial outcomes.
categories: Anchoring,BATNA,Criteria,Emotions,Identity and Core Values,Joint Gains,Power Dynamics,Principal-Agent Dynamics,Relationships,ZOPA
title: Battle for Horizon: Mezzo’s Bid to Build a Superhero Empire
oneline: A negotiation between Mezzo Publishing and Horizon Comics over a full cash acquisition of Horizon’s intellectual property and creative team to expand Mezzo’s superhero and fantasy portfolio.
